title: Null Hypothesis Significance Testing in Psychology
text:
Preface
I have heard some variant of this from many students.
I hear you! And I have some good news. You probably already know most of the math you need to succeed in this course. So don’t give up.

title: Null Hypothesis Significance Testing in Psychology
text:
As a General Student
If you are hoping to use this as an independent resource for learning statistics or coding fundamentals, this manual could provide a jumping off point. However, other sources will go into much greater depth. Remember, this is a lab manual meant to cover components of single class. As such, some material is intentionally incomplete and some examples are intentionally ambiguous. Recommendations for diving deeper:
Aron et al. (2013) “Statistics in Psychology”
McKinney (2022) “Python for Data Analysis”
Wickham and Grolemund (2017) “R for Data Science”
Beyond these specific sources, …

title: Null Hypothesis Significance Testing in Psychology
text:
As an Educator
If you are teaching statistics or within another field that involves extensive use of formulas, this manual could provide a template for making materials compliant with ADA Title II… Specifically, this…
Have existing LaTeX formulas? You can paste them into Quarto documents, add any additional material you would like (e.g., code), and render to HTML. With different rendering settings, you should be able to produce yeilds screen readable formulas.

title: Null Hypothesis Significance Testing in Psychology
text:
The Author
As of spring 2026, Garrett Greeley, Ph.D., is a Fixed-Term Assistant Professor in the Psychology Department at Winona State University.

title: Null Hypothesis Significance Testing in Psychology
text:
The Manual

Goal
This “lab manual” was inspired by a combination of existing resources. First, I have long been a fan of most…


Support
This project recieved support from the office of Academic and Student Affairs at Minnesota State through the Open Educational Resources (OER) “Learning Circles” program.


Format
This is a Quarto book. To learn more about Quarto books visit https://quarto.org/docs/books.


Notes
Generative AI was used for parts of this manual, including: code chunk authoring and grammar checking.




Aron, Arthur, J. Coups Elliot, and N. Aron Elaine. 2013. Statistics for Psychology. 6th ed. Pearson.


McKinney, Wes. 2022. Python for Data Analysis: Data Wrangling with Pandas, NumPy, and Jupyter. 3rd ed. O’Reilly Media.


Wickham, Hadley, and Garrett Grolemund. 2017. R for Data Science: Import, Tidy, Transform, Visualize, and Model Data. O’Reilly Media, Inc.

title: 1  Setup
text:
1.1 Getting Started with R
As part of this course, you have the ability to complete assignments (or parts of assignments) using R or Python. Getting set up with these tools is often a hurdle when first starting out. Python, in particular, could be a chore as there were many different ways to install the language itself and manage packages.
There are a few concepts to keep in mind when getting started with R:
These are sometimes conflated likely because of how intertwined they are. R itself is a statistical programming language, though it can do more than stats. R Studio is software that makes interacting with R (the language) more enjoyable. At present, Positron is another IDE that specializes in working with R (and Python). Specifically, it is built on top of VSCode (another IDE) with configurations baked in that are helpful for data science folks.
If you have no programming experience and want as simple of a setup as possible, I’d recommend R Studio.

2.2 Key Formulas
\[\bar{X} = \frac{\sum X}{N}\]
\[s^2 = \frac{\sum (X - \bar{X})^2}{N-1}\]
\[s = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (X - \bar{X})^2}{N-1}}\]
\[z = \frac{X - \bar{X}}{s}\]

title: 2  Central Tendency and Normalizing Data
text:
2.5 Exercises

2.5.1 Study Context
A researcher measured how high basketball players can jump (vertical leap), recorded in inches. Before testing, players were randomly assigned to drink either 12 oz of water or 12 oz of “Launch Sauce” (a new beverage aimed at enhancing athletic performance).
Water Group (n = 10)



Participant
Vertical (in.)




1
24


2
25


3
25


4
30


5
33


6
22


7
36


8
40


9
27


10
32



Launch Sauce Group (n = 12)



Participant
Vertical (in.)




1
20


2
33


3
38


4
29


5
34


6
28


7
26


8
37


9
41


10
30


11
25


12
41




water_group        <- c(24, 25, 25, 30, 33, 22, 36, 40, 27, 32)
launch_sauce_group <- c(20, 33, 38, 29, 34, 28, 26, 37, 41, 30, 25, 41)




2.5.2 Conceptual Questions

The formula for the mean of a sample is:
A. \(\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum X^2}{N}\)
B. \(\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum X}{N}\)
C. \(\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum X}{N-1}\)
D. \(\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum (X - \bar{X})}{N}\)
The formula for the variance of a sample is:
A. \(s^2 = \dfrac{\sum (X - \bar{X})}{N-1}\)
B. \(s^2 = \dfrac{\sum (X - \bar{X})^2}{N}\)
C. \(s^2 = \dfrac{\sum (X - \bar{X})^2}{N-1}\)
D. \(s^2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (X - \bar{X})^2}{N-1}}\)
The formula for the standard deviation of a sample is:
A. \(s = \dfrac{\sum (X - \bar{X})^2}{N-1}\)
B. \(s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (X - \bar{X})^2}{N}}\)
C. \(s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (X - \bar{X})^2}{N-1}}\)
D. \(s = \dfrac{\sum (X - \bar{X})^2}{N}\)
The formula for computing a z-score from a raw score is:
A. \(z = \dfrac{\bar{X} - X}{s}\)
B. \(z = \dfrac{X - \bar{X}}{s^2}\)
C. \(z = \dfrac{X - \bar{X}}{s}\)
D. \(z = X \cdot s + \bar{X}\)
The formula for computing a raw score from a z-score is:
A. \(X = \dfrac{z}{s} + \bar{X}\)
B. \(X = z \cdot s - \bar{X}\)
C. \(X = z \cdot s + \bar{X}\)
D. \(X = \dfrac{z - \bar{X}}{s}\)
Which of the following best describes what a z-score represents?
A. The raw score minus the median, expressed in units of variance
B. The number of standard deviations a score is above or below the mean
C. The probability that a score occurs in a normal distribution
D. The percentile rank of a score within a sample
If a value is exactly equal to the mean, its corresponding z-score will always be:
A. 1
B. Undefined
C. 0
D. Equal to the standard deviation
If a value is exactly one standard deviation above the mean, its z-score will always be:
A. 0
B. −1
C. 1
D. It depends on the sample size
TRUE or FALSE: If value “A” is 3.5 standard deviations above the mean, it is guaranteed that any randomly selected data point will be less than “A,” because z-scores max out at ±3.5.
A. True
B. False
TRUE or FALSE: Computing z-scores allows us to “normalize” data with respect to the mean, making it possible to compare performance across different outcomes (e.g., comparing a height score to a memory score).
A. True
B. False




2.5.3 Computational Questions
Use the study data above. Show all work.

Problem 1
Compute the variance and standard deviation for the Launch Sauce Group.



Statistic
Value




Sum =



N =



Mean =



SS =



Variance =



Standard Deviation =




 
 

Problem 2
Compute the z-score for a vertical of 30 inches in the Water Group. Show the mean, SD, and all computational steps.
 
 
 

Problem 3
Compute the z-score for a vertical of 37 inches in the Launch Sauce Group. Show all steps.
 
 
 

Problem 4
A z-score of 1.25 in the Launch Sauce Group corresponds to what raw score (round to the nearest inch)? Show all steps.
 
 
 

Problem 5
Answer the following probability questions. Assume a normal distribution.

What is the probability that a randomly selected score will be less than z = 0.0?

 

What is the probability that a randomly selected score will fall between z = −1 and z = +1?

 

What is the probability that a score in the Launch Sauce Group will be less than 21 inches? Show all steps.

 
 

Problem 6
Draw the approximately normal distribution implied by the Launch Sauce Group. On the x-axis, include two scales (raw scores and z-scores), with vertical lines at the mean and at ±1 and ±2 standard deviations. Label all key values.

title: 3  The Normal Distribution and Using Z Scores
text:
3.2 Key Formulas
\[z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}\]
\[SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\]
\[z = \frac{M - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}\]
crumbs: 3  The Normal Distribution and Using Z Scores

title: 3  The Normal Distribution and Using Z Scores
text: 3.3 Introduction…
crumbs: 3  The Normal Distribution and Using Z Scores

title: 3  The Normal Distribution and Using Z Scores
text:
3.4 Code Examples

library(palmerpenguins)
library(tidyverse)

mu    <- 45.1
sigma <- 5.5

penguins |>
  drop_na(bill_length_mm) |>
  mutate(z = (bill_length_mm - mu) / sigma) |>
  select(species, bill_length_mm, z) |>
  head(8)


pnorm(1.0)

z    <- 1.5
mu   <- 45.1
sigma <- 5.5
X    <- z * sigma + mu
X
crumbs: 3  The Normal Distribution and Using Z Scores

title: 3  The Normal Distribution and Using Z Scores
text:
3.5 Exercises

3.5.1 Conceptual Questions

The normal distribution is symmetric about its:
A. Mode only
B. Median only
C. Mean, median, and mode (all three are equal)
D. Variance
Approximately what percentage of scores in a normal distribution fall within ±1 standard deviation of the mean?
A. 50%
B. 68%
C. 95%
D. 99.7%
Approximately what percentage of scores in a normal distribution fall within ±2 standard deviations of the mean?
A. 50%
B. 68%
C. 95%
D. 99.7%
In a one-tailed test where the researcher predicts scores will be higher than average, the rejection region is located in:
A. The lower tail of the distribution
B. Both tails equally
C. The upper tail of the distribution
D. The center of the distribution
The null hypothesis for a z-test typically states:
A. The sample mean is significantly different from the population mean
B. There is no difference between the sample mean and the population mean
C. The sample mean is greater than the population mean
D. The population standard deviation equals zero
In a two-tailed test at α = .05, the rejection regions occupy:
A. The upper 5% of the distribution only
B. The lower 5% of the distribution only
C. 2.5% in each tail (5% total)
D. The central 5% of the distribution
The standard error of the mean (SE) is computed as:
A. \(SE = \sigma \cdot \sqrt{n}\)
B. \(SE = \dfrac{\sigma}{n}\)
C. \(SE = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\)
D. \(SE = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}\)
When α = .05, the critical z values for a two-tailed test are:
A. ±1.28
B. ±1.645
C. ±1.96
D. ±2.58
TRUE or FALSE: If the obtained z statistic falls beyond the critical value (into the rejection region), we reject the null hypothesis.
A. True
B. False
TRUE or FALSE: A two-tailed test is appropriate when the researcher has a specific directional prediction (e.g., predicts scores will increase).
A. True
B. False




3.5.2 Computational Questions
Reference Population Parameters



Variable
Mean (μ)
SD (σ)




Sleep
8 hours
1 hour


Intelligence
100 points
15 points


Height
68 inches
4 inches


Memory
20 items
5 items




populations <- list(
  sleep        = list(mean = 8,   sd = 1),
  intelligence = list(mean = 100, sd = 15),
  height       = list(mean = 68,  sd = 4),
  memory       = list(mean = 20,  sd = 5)
)



3.5.2.1 Part A: Drawing and Hypothesis Exercises
For each scenario: (a) draw and label the normal distribution implied by the relevant population parameters, and (b) write the null and alternative hypotheses in both symbolic and plain-language form.

Exercise 1 — Sleep
Dr. A is interested in whether Patient 47 is sleeping a different amount than the population average (in either direction).

Draw and label the distribution. (1 point)

 
 
 

Write H₀ and H₁. (2 points)

 
 

Exercise 2 — Intelligence
Dr. B is interested in whether a new drug (Intelli-Juice) improves intelligence scores compared to the known average.

Draw and label the distribution. (1 point)

 
 
 

Write H₀ and H₁. (2 points)

 
 

Exercise 3 — Height
Dr. C is interested in whether a new drug (Shrink-Capsules) reduces height compared to the known average.

Draw and label the distribution. (1 point)

 
 
 

Write H₀ and H₁. (2 points)

 
 

Exercise 4 — Memory
Dr. G is interested in whether exercise impacts Gavin’s memory in a way that is different from the known average (in either direction).

Draw and label the distribution. (1 point)

 
 
 

Write H₀ and H₁. (2 points)

 
 



3.5.2.2 Part B: z-Test Decision Problems
Assume α = .05 unless otherwise noted. Use the population parameters from the table above.

Problem 5 — Sleep (two-tailed)
A researcher is interested in whether N = 20 patients are getting significantly more or less sleep than average.

Is this a one- or two-tailed test? What is/are the cutoff point(s) at α = .05? (1 point)

 

What is/are the cutoff point(s) at α = .10? (1 point)

 

The sample slept an average of 9.8 hours. State your decision at α = .05 and interpret in a complete sentence; report p < .05 or p > .05. (1 point)

 
 

Problem 6 — Intelligence (one-tailed)
A researcher tests whether N = 100 members of a newly discovered alien species are super intelligent (i.e., score higher than average).

Is this a one- or two-tailed test? What is/are the cutoff point(s) at α = .05? (1 point)

 

What is/are the cutoff point(s) at α = .10? (1 point)

 

The alien sample averages 71 intelligence points. State your decision at α = .05 and interpret; report p < .05 or p > .05. (1 point)

 
 

Problem 7 — Height (one-tailed)
A researcher develops a “shrinking procedure” and measures resulting height for N = 553 participants.

Is this a one- or two-tailed test? What is/are the cutoff point(s) at α = .05? (1 point)

 

What is/are the cutoff point(s) at α = .01? (1 point)

 

The shrunken sample has an average height of 48 inches. State your decision at α = .05 and interpret; report p < .05 or p > .05. (1 point)

 
 

Problem 8 — Memory (two-tailed)
A researcher examines whether a new supplement changes memory performance in N = 1,500 volunteers.

Is this a one- or two-tailed test? What is/are the cutoff point(s) at α = .05? (1 point)

 

What is/are the cutoff point(s) at α = .01? (1 point)

 

After taking the supplement, the sample recalled an average of 23 items. State your decision at α = .05 and interpret; report p < .05 or p > .05. (1 point)

 
 

Bonus
What is the formula for a z-test (as opposed to a z-score for a single observation)?

title: 4  Hypothesis Testing: Core Concepts and Z Tests
text:
4.4 Code Examples

library(palmerpenguins)
library(tidyverse)

mu0   <- 200
sigma <- 14

adelie <- penguins |>
  filter(species == "Adelie") |>
  drop_na(flipper_length_mm)

M  <- mean(adelie$flipper_length_mm)
n  <- nrow(adelie)
SE <- sigma / sqrt(n)
z  <- (M - mu0) / SE
z


p <- 2 * pnorm(-abs(z))
p
crumbs: 4  Hypothesis Testing: Core Concepts and Z Tests

title: 4  Hypothesis Testing: Core Concepts and Z Tests
text:
4.5 Exercises

4.5.1 Conceptual Questions

In hypothesis testing, the null hypothesis (H₀) usually states that:
A. There is no effect or no difference
B. There is a difference in the expected direction
C. The researcher’s prediction is true
D. The effect size is large
The alternative hypothesis (H₁) in a one-tailed test states that:
A. The sample mean differs from the population mean in either direction
B. The sample mean is greater (or less) than the population mean, depending on prediction
C. The population mean equals the sample mean
D. Type I error is impossible
A Type I error occurs when:
A. You fail to reject H₀ when it is false
B. You reject H₀ when it is true
C. You reject H₁ when it is true
D. The p-value equals the effect size
A Type II error occurs when:
A. You reject H₀ when it is true
B. You fail to reject H₀ when it is false
C. α is greater than β
D. You set α = .05 instead of .01
Statistical power is defined as:
A. The probability of rejecting H₀ when H₀ is true
B. The probability of rejecting H₀ when H₀ is false
C. The probability of making a Type I error
D. The probability of failing to reject H₀
Which of the following increases statistical power?
A. Smaller sample size
B. Lower α (e.g., .01 vs. .05)
C. Larger sample size
D. More variability in the data
Which α level would make it harder to reject H₀?
A. α = .05
B. α = .10
C. α = .01
D. α = .50
A researcher predicts that caffeine improves memory recall. Which is the correct null hypothesis?
A. Caffeine improves recall
B. Caffeine impairs recall
C. Caffeine has no effect on recall
D. Caffeine improves recall more than nicotine
If a study has low power, what is most likely to happen?
A. More Type I errors
B. More Type II errors
C. More correct rejections of H₀
D. Effect size increases
The critical z value for a two-tailed test at α = .05 is:
A. ±1.28
B. ±1.645
C. ±1.96
D. ±2.58




4.5.2 Computational Questions
Note: if direction is not specified, use direction implied by hypothesis.
For each problem: write H₀ and H₁, identify the cutoff value(s), compute z (showing the SE calculation and all steps), state your decision, and interpret the result in plain language. Report p < .05 or p > .05 as appropriate.

Problem 1
A cognitive psychologist believes a new mnemonic strategy increases immediate recall above the population average. Use α = .05.



Parameter
Value




Population mean (μ₀)
15 items


Population SD (σ)
4


Sample size (n)
36


Sample mean (M)
16.5 items




mu0   <- 15     # population mean (items)
sigma <- 4      # population SD
n     <- 36     # sample size
M     <- 16.5   # sample mean (items)
alpha <- 0.05

 
 
 

Problem 2
An experiment measures reaction time. Use α = .05, two-tailed.



Parameter
Value




Population mean (μ₀)
350 ms


Population SD (σ)
60 ms


Sample size (n)
49


Sample mean (M)
340 ms




mu0   <- 350    # population mean (ms)
sigma <- 60     # population SD
n     <- 49     # sample size
M     <- 340    # sample mean (ms)
alpha <- 0.05

 
 
 

Problem 3
A hospital reviews case files and suspects that users of a particular drug die earlier than the national average. Use α = .01.



Parameter
Value




National mean lifespan (μ₀)
78 years


Population SD (σ)
6 years


Sample size (n)
64


Sample mean (M)
75.8 years




mu0   <- 78     # national mean lifespan (years)
sigma <- 6      # population SD
n     <- 64     # sample size
M     <- 75.8   # sample mean (years)
alpha <- 0.01

 
 
 

Problem 4
A counseling center uses a standardized depression score where lower scores indicate fewer symptoms. After a new group-therapy program, they assess a sample of clients. Use α = .10, two-tailed.



Parameter
Value




Population mean (μ₀)
22


Population SD (σ)
5


Sample size (n)
25


Sample mean (M)
20.2




mu0   <- 22     # population mean (lower = fewer symptoms)
sigma <- 5      # population SD
n     <- 25     # sample size
M     <- 20.2   # sample mean
alpha <- 0.10

 
 
 

Problem 5
A professor worries that a new, harder exam lowered student performance. Use α = .05.



Parameter
Value




Historical population mean (μ₀)
75


Population SD (σ)
10


Sample size (n)
100


Sample mean (M)
72

sigma <- 10     # population SD
n     <- 100    # sample size
M     <- 72     # sample mean

title: 5  T-Tests: Part #1
text:
5.2 Key Formulas
\[t = \frac{M - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{N}}\]
\[t = \frac{\bar{D}}{s_D / \sqrt{N}}\]
\[SE = \frac{s}{\sqrt{N}}, \qquad df = N - 1\]

title: 5  T-Tests: Part #1
text: 5.3 Introduction…

title: 5  T-Tests: Part #1
5  T-Tests: Part #1
text:
5.5 Exercises

5.5.1 Conceptual Questions

Dependent-measures t-tests should be used when:
A. Whenever possible
B. We have repeated measurements from the same participants (or matched pairs)
C. We are interested in a dependent variable
D. Our independent variable has two levels
A t-test is preferred over a z-test when we lack information about the:
A. Sample size
B. Population mean
C. Population standard deviation
D. Sample variance
The t-table provides different cutoff values of t for different:
A. Variances
B. Experimental designs
C. Degrees of freedom (N – 1)
D. Types of t-tests (independent vs. dependent)
TRUE or FALSE: The t distribution looks like the z (normal) distribution, but only when sample size is sufficiently large.
A. True
B. False
A data set has 100 participants, each contributing one response. The mean of the responses is 75.32. How many responses are free to vary?
A. 99
B. 100
C. 1
D. Not enough information to know




5.5.2 Computational Questions
Note: if direction is not specified, use direction implied by hypothesis.
For each problem: write H₀ and H₁, identify the cutoff value(s), compute the necessary descriptive statistics and t (showing all steps), state your decision, and interpret the result in plain language.

An athletic trainer believes a new training protocol increases golf swing speed. The historical population mean is μ₀ = 98 MPH, but the population standard deviation is unknown. N = 12 participants were tested after completing the protocol. Use α = .05.

Problem 3 — One-Sample t-Test
You are hired by the Minnesota Vikings to determine if they score a different number of points per game than the NFL average. Use α = .01.



Parameter
Value




Sample mean (M)
28 points


Sample SD
7


Sample size (N)
16 games


Population mean (μ₀)
24 points




mu0   <- 24   # NFL average points per game
M     <- 28   # Vikings sample mean
SD    <- 7    # Vikings sample SD
N     <- 16   # number of games
alpha <- 0.01

Write H₀/H₁, determine cutoff, compute t, decide, and interpret:
 
 
 

Problem 4 — One-Sample t-Test
A counseling center uses a standardized depression score (lower = fewer symptoms), with a known population mean of μ₀ = 22. Researchers expect the new group-therapy program to decrease scores. Use α = .10.



Parameter
Value




Population mean (μ₀)
22


Sample mean (M)
19.2


Sample SD
3.5


Sample size (N)
36




mu0   <- 22    # population mean (lower = fewer symptoms)
M     <- 19.2  # sample mean
SD    <- 3.5   # sample SD
N     <- 36    # sample size
alpha <- 0.10

Write H₀/H₁, determine cutoff, compute t, decide, and interpret:

title: 6  One-Way ANOVA
text: 6.1 Key Concepts

title: 6  One-Way ANOVA
text:
Normal Distribution
Variance, Standard Deviation
Probability Values
Cut-Off Points

title: 6  One-Way ANOVA
text:
6.2 Key Formulas
\[F = \frac{MS_{between}}{MS_{within}}\]
\[MS_{between} = \frac{SS_{between}}{df_{between}}, \qquad MS_{within} = \frac{SS_{within}}{df_{within}}\]
\[df_{between} = k - 1, \qquad df_{within} = N - k\]

title: 6  One-Way ANOVA
6  One-Way ANOVA
text:
6.4 Exercises

6.4.1 Conceptual Questions

The main purpose of a one-way ANOVA is to:
A. Test whether the variance of one sample equals another sample
B. Test whether three or more group means differ significantly, using variance information
C. Compare exactly two means
D. Measure the correlation between two variables
In a one-way ANOVA, the total variability in the data is divided into:
A. Within-group and between-group variance components
B. Experimental error and measurement bias
C. Main effects and interactions
D. Population variance and sampling variance
You are comparing exam performance across three different study methods. Which test is most appropriate?
A. One-sample t-test
B. Independent-means t-test
C. One-way ANOVA
D. Chi-square test
Why is it problematic to run multiple t-tests instead of an ANOVA when comparing 3 or more groups?
A. t-tests cannot handle unequal variances
B. t-tests reduce statistical power
C. Multiple t-tests inflate the Type I error rate
D. ANOVA is easier to compute
The correct null hypothesis for a one-way ANOVA with three groups is:
A. At least one group mean differs
B. All group means are equal
C. The variances across groups are equal
D. Each sample mean equals zero
The F statistic in ANOVA is always positive because:
A. Group means cannot differ in a negative direction
B. The denominator in F is always negative
C. Negative F values are omitted by convention
D. F is a ratio of squared quantities (variances)
Under the null hypothesis, the expected value of the F ratio is approximately:
A. 0
B. 1
C. −1
D. None of the above
An ANOVA yields F(2, 27) = 5.41, p = .010. Assuming α = .05, what should you conclude?
A. At least one group mean differs — reject the null
B. All group means are equal — fail to reject the null
C. Not enough information to decide
D. Each sample mean equals zero
An ANOVA yields F(2, 27) = 5.41, p = .010. How many groups are being compared?
A. 3
B. 4
C. 2
D. Not enough information to decide
An ANOVA yields F(2, 27) = 5.41, p = .010. How many participants were included in this analysis?
A. 3
B. 27
C. 30
D. Not enough information to decide




6.4.2 Computational Questions
Note: if direction is not specified, use direction implied by hypothesis.
For each problem:

Write out the null and alternative hypotheses
Find and report the critical/cutoff F value
Compute all intermediate values, showing steps (group means, SS between, SS within, df between, df within, MS between, MS within, F)
Complete an ANOVA summary table
Report the result: F(dfbetween, dfwithin) = F-value, p < or > α
Interpret the result in plain language


Problem 1
A researcher tests how puzzle solve-time (minutes) changes when people listen to different types of music. Fifteen participants were randomized into three conditions (n = 5 each). Use α = .05.

Popular

classical <- c(15, 14, 13, 16, 15)
popular   <- c(10, 12, 11, 9, 10)
alpha     <- 0.05

ANOVA Summary Table:

title: 7  Factorial ANOVA
text: 7.1 Key Concepts

title: 7  Factorial ANOVA
text:
Main Effects
Interactions
Factorial Design
Between-Group Variance

title: 7  Factorial ANOVA
text:
7.2 Key Formulas
\[F_A = \frac{MS_A}{MS_{within}}, \qquad F_B = \frac{MS_B}{MS_{within}}, \qquad F_{A \times B} = \frac{MS_{A \times B}}{MS_{within}}\]
\[df_A = a - 1, \qquad df_B = b - 1, \qquad df_{A \times B} = df_A \times df_B\]
\[df_{within} = N - (a \times b)\]

title: 7  Factorial ANOVA
7  Factorial ANOVA
text:
7.4 Exercises

7.4.1 Conceptual Questions

The main purpose of a factorial ANOVA is to:
A. Test whether the variance of one sample equals another sample
B. Test whether three or more group means differ significantly, using variance information
C. Test the main effects of two or more categorical variables and their interaction, using variance information
D. Measure the correlation between two variables
A factorial ANOVA requires computing ____, just like a one-way ANOVA:
A. SS between
B. SS within
C. MS between
D. All of the above
You are comparing how caffeine consumption (Placebo vs. 200 mg) impacts athletic performance (weight lifted), while also accounting for sleep quality (8 hours vs. 4 hours). Different participants are in each condition. This design calls for:
A. Repeated one-way ANOVAs
B. Repeated factorial ANOVAs
C. Pairwise t-tests
D. Factorial ANOVA
The study described in question 3 is best characterized as a:
A. 2×2×1 design
B. 2×2 factorial design
C. 4-factor design
D. Mixed factorial design
You are comparing how artistic expression (Drawing vs. Painting) impacts mood (self-reported happiness), while also accounting for diet (1, 2, or 3 meals per day). You find a significant main effect of artistic expression. This means:
A. The degrees of freedom are significant for that factor
B. The interaction cannot be statistically significant
C. The main effect of diet is unlikely to be statistically significant
D. None of the above
In the study described in question 5, happiness was higher following painting compared to drawing (p < .05), but only when participants ate one meal per day. This finding indicates:
A. There is a statistically significant interaction
B. There is a significant main effect of diet
C. There could be (but is not necessarily) a significant interaction
D. None of the above
In a factorial ANOVA, under the null hypothesis, the expected value of the F ratio for each main effect and the interaction is approximately:
A. 0
B. 1
C. −1
D. None of the above
A 2×4 factorial ANOVA yields F(3, 43) = 3.72 for the variable with 4 levels. Assuming α = .05, what should you conclude?
A. At least one group mean differs in that factor — reject the null
B. All group means are equal — fail to reject the null
C. Not enough information to decide
D. Each sample mean equals zero
You conduct a 2×3 factorial ANOVA. How many degrees of freedom does the interaction have?
A. 3
B. 4
C. 2
D. Not enough information to decide
A 2×2 factorial ANOVA has within-group degrees of freedom of 24. What is the total sample size?
A. 30
B. 28
C. 25
D. Not enough information to decide




7.4.2 Computational Questions
Use α = .05 for all problems.
For each problem:

Write out the null and alternative hypotheses for each main effect and the interaction
Find and report the critical F value for each effect
Compute df, MS, and F for each source, showing work
Complete the ANOVA summary table
Report each result: F(dfeffect, dfwithin) = F-value, p < or > α
Interpret each result in plain language

title: 8  Correlation
text: 8.1 Key Concepts

title: 8  Correlation
text:
Pearson Correlation
Spearman Correlation
Sum of Cross Products
Linear vs. Non-Linear Relationships

title: 8  Correlation
text:
8.2 Key Formulas
\[r = \frac{\sum(X - \bar{X})(Y - \bar{Y})}{\sqrt{SS_X \cdot SS_Y}}\]
\[t = r\sqrt{\frac{n-2}{1-r^2}}\]
\[df = n - 2\]

title: 8  Correlation
text:
8.3 Code Examples

library(palmerpenguins)
library(tidyverse)

penguins |>
  drop_na(bill_length_mm, flipper_length_mm) |>
  summarise(r = cor(bill_length_mm, flipper_length_mm))


penguins_clean <- penguins |> drop_na(bill_length_mm, flipper_length_mm)

cor.test(penguins_clean$bill_length_mm, penguins_clean$flipper_length_mm)

title: 8  Correlation
text:
8.4 Exercises

8.4.1 Conceptual Questions

The main purpose of a Pearson correlation is to:
A. Test the linear relationship between two continuous variables
B. Test the monotonic relationship between two continuous variables
C. Test how the means of two independent groups correlate (using variance information)
D. Test the generic association between two or more continuous variables
Like other tests we have covered, correlation also involves computing:
A. SS between
B. MS within
C. SS for each variable
D. All of the above
Why is it important to plot data when examining correlations?
A. There may be a non-linear relationship not properly characterized by a correlation coefficient
B. There could be a monotonic but not a linear relationship
C. There may be outliers
D. All of the above are reasonable answers
As X increases, so does Y — this is probably a:
A. Negative correlation
B. Positive correlation
C. Spearman rho
D. Inverse correlation
This correlation metric is less susceptible to outliers:
A. Spearman rho
B. Spearman ratio
C. Pearson r
D. F-ratio
Which of the following relationships is probably negatively correlated?
A. Hours slept and number of dreams had
B. Blood alcohol content and car accident probability
C. Hours slept and hours standing
D. None of the above; correlations cannot be less than 0
In a Pearson or Spearman correlation, under the null hypothesis, the expected value of r is approximately:
A. 0.1
B. 0.5
C. −1
D. 0
TRUE or FALSE: A researcher found that very little sleep (<5 hours) and very much sleep (10+ hours) are both associated with feeling miserable, while moderate sleep (7–9 hours) is associated with feeling good. This U-shaped relationship between sleep and mood is best described as linear.
A. True
B. False
TRUE or FALSE: If the correlation between X and Y is r = 0.5, then the correlation between Y and X will be r = −0.5.
A. True
B. False
Daily temperature is measured in both Fahrenheit and Celsius for an entire year. When you correlate the two scales:
A. They are perfectly correlated (r = 1.0)
B. Plotting them, all points fall on a perfect diagonal line
C. They are perfectly linearly related
D. All of the above




8.4.2 Computational Questions
Note: if direction is not specified, use direction implied by hypothesis.
For each problem:

Compute all intermediate values step by step: means, deviations, squared deviations, and the sum of cross products
Fill in the computation table
Compute Pearson r and the corresponding t statistic
Find and report the critical t value
Report the result: r(df) = r-value, p < or > α
Interpret the result in plain language

\[r = \frac{\sum(X - \bar{X})(Y - \bar{Y})}{\sqrt{SS_X \cdot SS_Y}} \qquad t = r\sqrt{\frac{n-2}{1-r^2}} \qquad df = n - 2\]

Y <- c(12, 19,  9, 6, 21)   # exam score
alpha <- 0.05

title: 9  Regression
text: 9.1 Key Concepts

title: 9  Regression
text:
Simple Linear Regression
Multiple Regression
Slope and Intercept
Predicted Values (Ŷ)

title: 9  Regression
text:
9.2 Key Formulas
\[\hat{Y} = bX + a\]
\[b = r \cdot \frac{s_Y}{s_X}, \qquad a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{X}\]

title: 9  Regression
text:
9.3 Code Examples

library(palmerpenguins)
library(tidyverse)

penguins_clean <- penguins |> drop_na(bill_length_mm, flipper_length_mm)

lm(flipper_length_mm ~ bill_length_mm, data = penguins_clean) |> summary()


penguins_clean <- penguins |> drop_na(bill_length_mm, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g)

lm(flipper_length_mm ~ bill_length_mm + body_mass_g, data = penguins_clean) |> summary()

title: 9  Regression
text:
9.4 Exercises

9.4.1 Conceptual Questions

Simple regression provides additional information above and beyond correlation, namely estimates for:
A. Strength and direction
B. Slope and intercept
C. Relationship and distance
D. Non-linear dynamics
The slope estimate in a regression formula is the:
A. Alpha
B. Beta ratio
C. “X” term in the formula
D. Beta coefficient
Regression determines the characteristics of a line that best fits through the data. Specifically, this line:
A. Intersects the x-axis when data values are positive
B. Intersects as many data points as possible
C. Maximizes the sum of the vertical distances between the data and the line
D. Minimizes the sum of the squared vertical distances between the data and the line
Ŷ (Y-hat) is:
A. The predicted Y value based on the regression formula
B. The observed Y value based on the regression formula
C. Inversely correlated with observed Y values
D. Not a value related to regression at all
TRUE or FALSE: Multiple regression can accommodate predictor variables of various types, including categorical predictors.
A. True
B. False
Consider the formula Ŷ = bX. What is missing, if anything?
A. The slope
B. The intercept
C. The predictor variable
D. Nothing is missing
Under the null hypothesis, the expected value of a beta coefficient is approximately:
A. 0
B. 0.5
C. −1
D. 0.1
Regression is BLUE. What does that mean?
A. Best Non-Linear Unbiased Estimate
B. Best Line Under Expected Value
C. Big Line Unsure Equivalence
D. Best Linear Unbiased Estimate
TRUE or FALSE: Multiple regression can accommodate two categorical predictors and no continuous predictors.
A. True
B. False
Dr. B conducts a regression predicting conference attendance (number of days) from attendee age (in years). Assuming age is not normalized into z-scores, the intercept will be:
A. Not computable, but would be sensical
B. Computable, but non-sensical
C. Not computable, but sensical
D. None of the above




9.4.2 Computational Questions
Note: show all steps. For each sub-part, plug the given values into the formula and compute Ŷ.

title: 10  Summary
text: In summary, this book has no content whatsoever.

text:

for Psychology. 6th ed. Pearson.

Pandas, NumPy, and Jupyter. 3rd ed. O’Reilly Media.
